coredata.dc:title: Pitch-derived carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of ferric ions and bioimaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yuehui
Shi, Yantao
Song, Xuedan
Zhao, Zhengyan
Zhang, Naitian
Hao, Ce
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) emerging as newcomers of carbon nanoparticles exhibit fascinating electro-optical properties, and thereby have been widely used in many fields. Herein, we present a new facile, economical and environmentally attractive strategy for the preparation of CQDs via one-step ozone (O3) treatment of coal tar pitch (CTP). CQDs prepared by this approach exhibit a narrow size distribution between 3–7 nm, and CQDs with a diameter of 4.8 nm accounts for approximately 42.8 %. CQDs were investigated as fluorescent probes for the detection of metal ions, and demonstrated highly sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions. A calibration curve displayed a linear response over the range of 0–9 μM and a detection limit of 0.02 μM (1.12 ppb). Moreover, the photoluminescence performance of the CQDs/Fe3+ complex could be recovered with the addition of EDTA, indicating a great potential for applications of this unique fluorescent CQDs in the detection of Fe3+. Finally, the experiments of CQDs in cell imaging confirmed that the CQDs can be used as potential fluorescent probes in biological imaging, cell labeling with strong luminescence and low toxicity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Coal tar pitch
Ozone oxidation
Fe3+detection
Biological imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of metal ions with CQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21677029 and 21606040 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of CQDs using CTP as precursor. (b) TEM and (c) HRTEM images of the CQDs (insets: FFT patterns). (d) the overall size distributions of CQDs by Dynamic light scattering (DLS).

(a) The spectra of the CTP (blue line) and CQDs (red line). (b) Full XPS survey spectea of the CTP (blue line) and CQDs (red line). (c) C 1s spectra of the CTP. (d) O 1s spectra of the CTP. (e) C 1s spectra of the CQDs. (f) O 1s spectra of the CQDs.

(a) UV–vis and PL spectra of CQDs in aqueous solution (Inset shows photographs of CQDs suspension under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right)). (b) PL spectra of CQDs aqueous solution at different excitation wavelength (300 to 480 nm). (c) Fluorescence intensity ratios (F/F 0) of the CQDs after different metal ions adding. (d) PL spectra of CQDs with different concentration of Fe3+ adding (excitation at 340 nm). (e) The plot of F 0 -F versus the different concentration of Fe3+ ions. (Inset shows the linear plot (Fe3+ concentration ranging from 0 to 9 μM) for Fe3+ detection). (f) The linear plot of F 0 /F versus the different concentration of Fe3+ ions (Fe3+ concentration ranging from 0 to 9 μM).

(a) (b) Fluorescence decay curves of CQDs in aqueous solution before and after Fe3+ adding. (c) Zeta potential distribution of the CQDs in aqueous solution. (d) Zeta potential distribution of the CQDs in the presence of Fe3+. (e) The overall size distributions of the CQDs before and after the Fe3+ adding. (f) Fluorescence quenching mechanism of the raw CQDs with the Fe3+ adding.

(a) Cell viability of J774A.1 cells incubated with CQDs for 24 h. Microscopic images of J774A.1 cells after incubation with CQDs in the absence and present of Fe3+ by excitation at 488 nm (b) (e), under bright field (c) (f), and overlay (d) (g).
Comparison of various quantum dots fluorescent probes for the detection of Fe3+.

head.ce:title: Pitch-derived carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of ferric ions and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Department of Chemistry, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian, 116024, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Department of Chemistry, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, People's Republic of China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) emerging as newcomers of carbon nanoparticles exhibit fascinating electro-optical properties, and thereby have been widely used in many fields. Herein, we present a new facile, economical and environmentally attractive strategy for the preparation of CQDs via one-step ozone (O3) treatment of coal tar pitch (CTP). CQDs prepared by this approach exhibit a narrow size distribution between 3–7 nm, and CQDs with a diameter of 4.8 nm accounts for approximately 42.8 %. CQDs were investigated as fluorescent probes for the detection of metal ions, and demonstrated highly sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions. A calibration curve displayed a linear response over the range of 0–9 μM and a detection limit of 0.02 μM (1.12 ppb). Moreover, the photoluminescence performance of the CQDs/Fe3+ complex could be recovered with the addition of EDTA, indicating a great potential for applications of this unique fluorescent CQDs in the detection of Fe3+. Finally, the experiments of CQDs in cell imaging confirmed that the CQDs can be used as potential fluorescent probes in biological imaging, cell labeling with strong luminescence and low toxicity.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), newcomers of carbon nanoparticles physically defined by diameter of less than 10 nm, exhibit some attractive physical, chemical and biological properties owing to their unique quantum confinement and edge effects [1,2]. In addition to photoluminescence (PL) property, CQDs are more advantageous in terms of cost, cytotoxicity, and biocompatibility as compared to those semiconductor quantum dots like PbS, CdS, CdSe and so on [3]. So far, CQDs have been widely used in biological imaging, cell sensing, light emitting diodes, organic photovoltaic devices, photocatalysis and electrocatalysis [4–6]. Sarkar et al. prepared dendrimer functionalized CQDs from sweet lemon peel, which are successfully used in the fields of selective detection of breast cancer and gene therapy [7]. Tang’s research demonstrated that the synthesized carbon dots (CDs) possess unique hole-accepting nature, promoting selective CO2 reduction to CH3OH by pure water [8]. To date, CQDs have been synthesized by means of hydrothermal approach, ultrasonic treatment, laser etching, microwave treatment, electrochemical or chemical oxidation, and so on [9]. The precursors are various carbon resources like graphene, fullerene, carbon black, carbon fibre, graphite, biomass and others [10]. For example, coal has been validated to be an economical carbon resource for preparation of CQDs through chemical oxidation. Related research works have been reported by Ye et al. who synthesized CQDs by oxidation of coal using H2SO4 and HNO3 [11,12]. Based on the same strategy, CQDs with single-layer graphene structure have been synthesized successfully by Dong and co-workers when using coal as precursor [13]. Whereas, most of the current approaches for preparation of CQDs are still confronted with the problems of complicated synthetic processes or harsh synthetic conditions. For example, the oxidation approach mentioned above employing H2SO4/HNO3 acid as the oxidizing agent is not environmentally friendly and can be a source of environmental pollution after excessive oxidation. Therefore, the exploration of low-cost raw materials, green and facile method to prepare CQDs is highly desired.

Coal tar pitch (CTP), as an industrial by-product of coal carbonization, is abundant and economically worldwide. At present, it is primarily used for paving roads and paint manufacturing. The organic molecules in CTP are usually composed of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons with edge alkyl side chains or heteroatomic functional groups [14,15]. CTP as a low-cost carbon source can be developed into many functional carbon materials like fullerene, carbon nanotubes, and graphene that demonstrate great potential in many important applications such as physics, chemistry, electronics, and biology [16,17]. Jeong Goo et al. synthesized the carbon nanotubes (CNT) via using CTP with chemical vapor deposition (CVD) method [18]. Very recently, Zhang et al. reported the use of CTP as natural CQDs, which were then validated to be good candidate for photodegradation of rhodamine B when coupled with TiO2 [19].

Herein, for the first time we report a novel one-step ozone oxidation strategy for the preparation of CQDs using CTP, which is facile and environmentally attractive as compared with HNO3/H2SO4 oxidation. The CQDs exhibit a narrow size distribution between 3–7 nm, among which the CQDs with a diameter of 4.8 nm account for approximately 42.8 %. Furthermore, the CQDs were applied to the detection of metal ions, which exhibit a high selectivity and sensitivity for Fe3+ detection, with detection limit as low as 0.02 μM. Moreover, the PL performance of the CQDs/Fe3+ complex can be recovered by adding EDTA, indicating a great potential in the detection of Fe3+ ions. Finally, the experiments of CQDs in cytotoxicity and cellular imaging confirmed that the CQDs are nontoxic and well biocompatible.

Coal tar pitch (CTP) was obtained from Steel Co., Ltd. (An Shan, China). FeCl3·6H2O, NaCl, Pb(NO3)2, AgNO3, Al(NO3)3, CaCl2, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, Hg(ClO4)2, CoCl2·6H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, KNO3, BaCl2, MgSO4, Ni(NO3)2, ZnCl2, EDTA, quinine sulphate and phosphate buffer solution (PBS) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All reagents used in the experiment were analytical reagent grade.

The CTP was milled into a powder using an agate mortar and dried before the experiment. In a typical reaction, CTP (3 g) and deionized water (150 mL) were added to a ball-mill machine (500 rpm for 4 h) to obtain a water solution of CTP. O3 concentration was kept at 75 mg L–1 by ozone generator with O2 as oxygen source, and a brown solution was obtained after O3 oxidation for 2 h at 35 °C. Then, the CQDs were collected through centrifugation at 18,000 rpm for 30 min twice. The concentration was estimated to be approximately 0.5 mg mL–1 from the weight of the dried CQDs.

The structure of the CQDs was measured using High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Tecnai F30). The overall size distribution of the CQDs aqueous solution was determined by using a Zeta sizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments, U.K.). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, NOVA nanoSEM 450) was used to characterize the morphological characteristics of CTP. The analysis of functional groups in CTP and CQDs was conducted using a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, iN10) spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB250) was carried out on non-monochromatic Mg-Ka radiation to determine the chemical bonding configurations of the CQDs. The UV–vis absorption of the CQDs aqueous solution was performed using a Cary WinUV spectrophotometer (Agilent), and the photoluminescence spectrum of the CQDs was obtained with a F97 Pro fluorescence spectrometer (China). The luminescence spectrometer (OB 920, Edinburgh Instruments Ltd, U.K.) was used to determine the fluorescence lifetimes. Cellular fluorescence images were obtained on confocal laser fluorescence microscope (Olympus, FV1000, Japan). The O3 was generated with ozone generator (QJ-8010 K, Guangzhou quanju Ozone Technology Co., Ltd, China).

The QY of the CQDs aqueous solutions was obtained using quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference (QY = 0.54 at 340 nm). Then, the fluorescence QY was calculated with the following formula:
(1)
φ =
φ s
×
I
I s
×
A s
A
×
η 2
η s 2
Where φ indicates the fluorescence QY of the CQDs aqueous solutions; A indicates the UV–Vis absorption at the excitation wavelength of CQDs; I is the integral area under the fluorescence curves of CQDs; η is solvent refractive index; s indicates the reference data.

To further gain insight into the photoluminescence (PL) decay behavior, a multidimensional time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique was employed to evaluate the PL decay lifetime of CQDs and CQDs-Fe3+, The decay traces were fitted with bi-exponential functions y (t) in formula (2):
(2) y(t)=A1 exp(-t/τ1 )+A2 exp(-t/τ2 )
where A1 and A2 (A1 + A2 = 1) are the fractional contributions of time resolved decay lifetimes of τ1 and τ2 , respectively. And the average lifetime τave can be calculated according to formula (3):
(3)
τ
a v e
=
A 1
τ 1 2
+
A 2
τ 2 2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of metal ions with CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: An Fe3+ aqueous solution (10–4 mol·L–1) was prepared in advance, and different volumes of Fe3+ solution were added to a 3 mL CQDs aqueous solution. After stabilization for approximately 30 s, the PL intensity of the CQDs was recorded with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

To determine the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the as-prepared CQDs, different metal solutions (10–4 mol·L–1) such as Ag+, K+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Ba2+,Mg2+, Na+, Co2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, and all the ions mixture solutions as interfering ions were added to the CQDs under identical conditions. The concentrations of the interfering ions were the same as that of Fe3+ concentration.

To explore the performance of the CQDs-Fe3+ towards EDTA, Fe3+ solution was added until fluorescence quenching occurred. Then, different volumes of EDTA solution (10–3 mol·L–1) were added to the CQDs-Fe3+ system, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded under identical conditions.

To evaluate the cytotoxicity of CQDs, J774A.1 cells were incubated in DEME medium for 24 h at 37 °C with 5% CO2. The medium was replaced by the CQDs with the different concentrations (0, 10, 20, 50, 100, and 200 μg mL–1) for 24 h, and the cells were washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution. Then MTT (5 mg mL–1) was dissolved in PBS solution, the optical density and the cell viability were calculated by MTT method for another 4 h.

J774A.1 cells were incubated in DEME medium for 24 h at 37 °C with 5% CO2. The medium was replaced by CQDs solution (200 μg mL–1) and incubated for 24 h, and the Fe3+ ions (2 mM) was added to the cells after 4 h. Then cellular fluorescence images of were obtained on confocal laser fluorescence microscope under the excitation condition of 488 nm.

Ozone oxidation is an approach featuring non-toxic, non-polluting, treatable and environmentally friendly when compared with HNO3/H2SO4 oxidation, which has been widely used in wastewater treatment [20–22]. As schematically described in Fig. 1 a, the CQDs were prepared by ozone treatment of CTP and purified using a high speed centrifuge (see details in experimental section). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) characterization (Fig. S1) shows that the sizes and shapes of CTP were irregular, ranging from approximately 10 μm–30 μm in diameter. Blue fluorescence emitted from the as-prepared CQDs aqueous solution can be observed when irradiated with UV light (Fig. 1a). The size and morphological distribution of the CQDs were measured by TEM and DLS analysis. The TEM image in Fig. 1b confirm the successful synthesis of CQDs, whose diameters are definitely below 10 nm. The DLS result (Fig.1d) illustrates that the CQDs have a narrow size distribution between 3–7 nm, in which the quantity of CQDs with a diameter of about 4.8 nm account for about 42.8 %. HRTEM image in Fig. 1c clearly shows the lattice of CQDs, and the fourier transform (FFT) pattern suggests that the CQDs have a hexagonal crystalline structure corresponding to the (100) graphitic inplane lattice [23].

The chemical structures of CQDs and raw CTP were characterized by FT-IR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As displayed in Fig. 2 a, the peaks at 3393 cm–1 and 3030 cm–1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH, COOH, and the bending vibrations of CH bonds in the aromatic hydrocarbon, respectively. The peaks at 1597 cm–1 and 1450 cm–1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of CC in the raw CTP. The peaks at 1726 cm–1 and 1263 cm–1 are attributed to CO and COC stretching vibrations, respectively. From the results of FT-IR, we can see that the chemical structure from CTP to CQDS has changed obviously. As a result of ozone oxidation, the contents of oxygen-containing groups on CQDs, including OH, CO and COC, increase dramatically when compared to the raw CTP, suggesting that the phenyl groups of CTP can be robustly oxidized by ozone treatment. Accordingly, the enrichment of surface hydrophilic groups (OH and COOH) enables CQDs to be well dispersed in water, which would allow these materials to be used in photocatalysis or biochemical studies [24,25].

Fig. 2b shows the full XPS spectra for CTP and CQDs. The two peaks corresponding to the graphitic C1 s peak at 284.6 eV and the O1 s peak at 532.3 eV can be clearly observed, while the N1 s peak at 400.8 eV is not very pronounced by comparison [26]. The oxygen contents in CQDs has increased remarkably by comparison to CTP. To be specific, the proportions of C, O, and N elements in CTP are determined to be about 96.0 %, 2.2 %, and 1.8 %, respectively; while that for CQDs are about 60.9 %, 36.6 %, and 2.5 %, respectively. The possible chemical species of each main element are further analyzed via the deconvolution of the C and O peaks. As displayed in Fig. 2c, the C1 s peak of CTP can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 284.6 eV and 286.7 eV that correspond to CC/CC/C–H and C-O-C/COH species, respectively. It is evident that the C1 s peak of CQDs is different from that of CTP. Three peaks can be observed in the deconvolution of the C1 s peak of CQDs (Fig. 2e), and the peak of CO at 288.6 eV is presumably due to the formation of epoxide and carbonyl. Consistent findings are the oxygen species in CTP and CQDs presented by XPS, as shown in Fig. 2d and f. In addition to the common peak corresponding to COC/COH at about 532.7 eV, there is an additional peak at 531.3 eV deconvoluted from the O1 s peaks of CQDs (Fig. 2f), which corresponds to CO. The above results obtained from the XPS spectra are matched with the FT-IR results, confirming that the surface of CQDs is rich in oxygen-containing functional groups, including OH, CO, and COC groups owing to ozone oxidation.

The intrinsic light absorption and emission behavior were analyzed by UV–vis spectra and photoluminescence (PL) spectra. In the UV–vis spectra of CQDs in aqueous dispersion (Fig. 3 a), an absorption can be observed at about 250 nm, an indicator of the typical absorption of aromatic bonds belonging to CC* and conversion of π-π*, which is in accordance with the result of previous studies [27]. In the PL spectra, the CQDs aqueous dispersion exhibits PLE and PL peaks at about λ =340 nm and λ =450 nm, respectively. And a blue fluorescence (inset in Fig. 3a) can be observed under a hand-held UV lamp (λ =365 nm). The photoluminescence QY of the CQDs was measured to be about 1.3 % under a UV light excitation (λ =340 nm) when using quinine sulphate acid as a standard [28]. Compared with the reported CQDs prepared by strong oxidants (concentrated H2SO4, HNO3, KMnO4, etc.), a lower QY of our CQDs sample may be attributed to the relatively lower oxidation degree due to the simple and mild O3 oxidation method used in this work. On the other hand, the QY of CQDs may be also related to the pH value. In our work, the pH value for the application of our CQDs in bioimaging is about 7.0. From Fig. S2, we can see that the PL intensity at pH = 7.0 is moderate as compared to that measured at pH = 3.0, 4.0 or 5.0. The mechanism could be more complex and need a further investigation in future.

It is found that the PL wavelength changes when the CQDs aqueous was excited at different wavelength in the range of 300–480 nm. A gradual increase in the PL intensity can be observed at excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 340 nm, followed by a gradual decrease from 340 to 480 nm. In this measurement, a maximum PL intensity was achieved at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm (Fig. 3b). The results imply the dependence of the PL intensity on the excitation wavelengths, which is a result of the surface state of the CQDs [29,30]. The PL of the CQDs was also measured at different pH values (Fig. S2). It is found that the PL intensity of the CQDs is relatively stronger under acidic conditions, which however will decrease remarkably under strong basic conditions. This behavior confirms that our CQDs possess pH dependence, the reason why the CQDs can be well dispersed in water is related to the surface functional groups and charged characteristics. Different pH values will lead to protonation or deprotonation on the surface of CQDs, which affects the surface charge and even the degree of aggregation of CQDs. The particle size distributions at different pH values were characterized by DLS (Fig. S3), and the average particle sizes at pH = 1.0, pH = 7.0, pH = 12.0 are 37.8 nm, 4.8 nm, and 0.6 nm, respectively. Therefore, we speculate that the CQDs are inclined to aggregate at acid condition, a negative factor for PL enhancement. On the other hand, we can conjecture that aggregation of CQDs should be suppressed or even the structure of CQDs could be destroyed by being dissolved at basic condition where CQDs are deprotonated, finally resulting in the decline of PL intensity.

The oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CQDs can act as ligands to coordinate with metal ions in solution, whereby CQDs may be used in the detection of metal ions in solution. In biological tissues, Fe ions exist in the forms of Fe2+ and Fe3+and plays a crucial role in enzymatic catalysis and cellular metabolism. However, high concentrations of iron are harmful to human cells and tissues [31,32]. At present, the preferred methods for the determination of Fe3+ ions in solution including ICP-MS, atomic absorption spectrometry, spectrophotometry and others, which require special instruments and complex
operation, limiting their application in the life science [33–35]. We firstly investigated the selectivity of the CQDs aqueous dispersion towards 17 metal

ions, including Ag+, Pb2+, Al3+, Co2+, Mg2+, Cr2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, K+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Na+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Zn2+ Fe2+, Fe3+. As illustrated in Fig. 3c, only Fe3+ exhibits a significant fluorescence declining significantly, suggesting that the CQDs has a good selectivity for detection of Fe3+ ions. Fig. 3d displays the PL spectra of the CQDs aqueous solution with Fe3+ concentrations ranging from 0 to 23 μM, in which the PL intensity decreased after the Fe3+ adding. And the PL intensity is found to be quenched linearly with the Fe3+ concentration from 0 to 9 μM (Fig. 3e). A linear regression analysis produces the expression F 0 – F = 131.391c + 76.247, with R 2 = 0.997, where F 0 and F are the PL intensities before and after Fe3+adding. The theoretical detection limit (LOD) of the CQDs for Fe3+ was calculated to be 0.02 μM (1.12 ppb) according to a LOD = 3σ/k [36], where σ is 1.08, the standard deviation of the blank solution (n = 9), and k is the slope of the linear response region range from 0 to 9 μM. Meanwhile, several concentration values close to the LOD values (0.05 μM, 0.1 μM, 0.2 μM) were measured as shown in Fig. S4. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity changed slightly. Above results imply that our CQDs are very sensitive in detection of Fe3+ ions. We further explored the anti-interference ability of the CQDs on the detection of Fe3+. As shown in Fig. S5, the PL quenching occurs by adding 23 μM Fe3+ (black line), while there is only slight decrease when other metal ions with the same concentration are added (red line), indicating that the CQDs aqueous dispersion have a strong anti-interference performance. Interestingly, the PL intensity of CQDs can even be completely restored when EDTA is added into the dispersion (red line), as shown in Fig. S6, suggesting that the PL intensity of CQDs used in fluorescent probe can be regenerated after the EDTA adding.

To better understand the PL quenching mechanism after adding Fe3+ ions, the fluorescence quenching was fitted using the Stern-Volmer equation by F0/F = 1+Ksv [Fe3+], where F0 and F refer to the fluorescence intensities of CQDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions respectively; Ksv represents the static Stern-Volmer constants. The analysis of the Stern − Volmer plots in Fig. 3e showed that they followed a linear trend with a constant Ksv equal to be 0.0697 μM (6.97 × 104 M−1). According to the double molecular quenching constant (kq ), kqτ0 = ksv , τ0 was already known, thus kq was 1.2 × 1013 M−1 s−1, which is much higher than the collision-controlled limit (1010 M−1 s−1) for the quenching fluorescence molecules. The observed quenching could be static quenching, and was caused by the formation of non-fluorescent complexes between the functional groups of CQDs and Fe3+ [37]. Meanwhile, the PL decay curves of CQDs and CQDs-Fe3+ aqueous dispersion were measured and fitted using a dual-exponential equation, as shown in Fig. 4 a and b. The calculated mean PL lifetime of the CQDs in the absence and in the presence of Fe3+ ion is 7.64 ns and 7.33 ns, respectively, as shown in Table S1, indicating no significant change of PL lifetime when Fe ions were presented. Here, we conjecture that it is because a small fraction of CQDs did not coordinate to Fe3+ ions, thus there should be no change of PL lifetime. Fig. 4c shows that the zeta potential of the CQDs in aqueous dispersion was -21.8 mV, suggesting that the CQDs’ surface is negatively charged, which is possibly due to the deprotonation of carboxyl or hydroxyl groups. It is obvious that, after adding Fe3+, there is a positive shift of zeta potential from −21.8 mV to −18.4 mV (Fig. 4d). According to previous reports, the fact that the zeta potential becomes more positive is possibly because the deprotonated carboxyl or hydroxyl groups coordinate with the Fe3+ cation, thereby leading to the charge neutralization [38–40]. Moreover, the size of the CQDs is about 4.8 nm, while the size of the CQDs after Fe3+ addition is about 594.3 nm (Fig. 4e), which indicates that the addition may also lead to aggregation, a combined mechanism responsible for the PL quenching. As schematically depicted in Fig. 4f, electron transition takes place as the CQDs is irradiated with UV light (λ =365 nm), namely from the ground states (S0) to the excited states (S1). For the CQDs system, radiation recombination can happen as some of the excited electrons on S1 return to S0, leading to the emission of blue fluorescence. However, when Fe3+ is added to the CQDs system, the electrons on S 1 could be transferred to Fe3+ cation instead of releasing photons through a radiative way. Based on the above results, it is inferred that the complexation and electron transfer between Fe3+ and the CQDs led to the occurrence of PL quenching.

The performance of various QDs fluorescent probes is compared in Table 1 , where it can be seen that the main synthesis methods include the hydrothermal method, microwave method, and others. The synthesis of CQDs has focused on various carbon resources, including citric acid, aminosalicylic acid, dopamine hydrochloride and so on, and the LOD of our CQDs is much lower than most of those previously reported ones. These results suggested that our present synthesis method has potential for use in the preparation of CQDs as a facile, inexpensive, and environmentally attractive strategy, and may be a promising method for Fe3+ detection in aqueous solutions.

For further explore the applications of CQDs in biological imaging, the J774A.1 cells experiment were used to evaluate the cytotoxicity. Fig.5 a, displays the cells were incubated under different concentrations of CQDs from 10 μg mL–1 to 200 μg mL–1. About 85 % of the original cell viability can retain after the cells were incubated with 200 μg mL–1of the CQDs for 24 h, suggesting that our CQDs are low toxic and can be used in cell imaging. Moreover, confocal laser scanning microscopy was carried out to evaluate the application of cell imaging. The J774A.1 cells were incubated with 200 μg mL–1 of CQDs for 4 h. As shown in Fig. 5b, cells after incubation with CQDs exhibit blue fluorescence on the cytoplasmic area under the excitation condition of 488 nm. However, when the cells were incubated with Fe3+ for 4 h, the blue fluorescence declines significantly, suggesting that the cellular environment does not affect the binding of CQDs and Fe3+ ions. These results confirmed that CQDs were low toxicity and well biocompatibility.

In summary, using CTP as carbon precursor, we for the first time report ozone oxidation to be a new facile, economical and environmentally attractive strategy for the preparation of CQDs. The CQDs prepared by this approach have abundant surface oxygen-containing function groups, and exhibit a narrow size distribution. The CQDs can be used in the detection of metal ions, and demonstrated highly sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions. A calibration curve displayed a linear response over the range of 0–9 μM and a detection limit of 0.02 μM (1.12 ppb), much lower than that reported previously. Moreover, the PL performance of the CQDs/Fe3+ complex could be recovered with the addition of EDTA, indicating great potential for applications of this unique fluorescent CQDs in the detection of Fe3+. Finally, the experiments of CQDs in cell imaging confirmed that the CQDs synthesized by ozone oxidation of CTP can be used as potential fluorescent probes in biological imaging, cell labeling with strong luminescence and low toxicity.
Yuehui Li: Data curation, writing-original draft preparation.
Yantao Shi: Writing-Reviewing and Editing.
Xuedan Song: Supervision, Visualization and investigation.
Zhengyan Zhao: Software and validation.
Naitian Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology
Ce Hao: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21677029 and 21606040).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2021.113253.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.N. Baker, G.A. Baker, Luminescent carbon nanodots: emergent nanolights, Angew Chem Int Ed. 49 (2010) 6726-6744.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots-Emerging light emitters for bioimaging, cancer therapy and optoelectronics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Hola, Y. Zhang, Y. Wang, E.P. Giannelis, R. Zboril, A.L. Rogach, Carbon dots-Emerging light emitters for bioimaging, cancer therapy and optoelectronics, Nano Today. 9 (2014) 590-603.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in graphene quantum dots: synthesis, properties, and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Haque, J. Kim, V. Malgras, K.R. Reddy, A.C. Ward, J. You, Y. Bando, M.S.A. Hossain, Y. Yamauchi, Recent Advances in Graphene Quantum Dots: Synthesis, Properties, and Applications, Small Methods. 2 (2018) 1800050.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dot-based composites for catalytic applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.B. Chen, M.L. Liu, C.Z. Huang, Carbon dot-based composites for catalytic applications, Green Chemistry. 22 (2020) 4034-4054.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in energy conversion applications of carbon dots: from optoelectronic devices to electrocatalysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Feng, S. Tao, D. Yue, Q. Zeng, W. Chen, B. Yang, Recent Advances in Energy Conversion Applications of Carbon Dots: From Optoelectronic Devices to Electrocatalysis, Small. 16 (2020) 2001295.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanodots in photodynamic antimicrobial therapy: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Knoblauch, C.D. Geddes, Carbon Nanodots in Photodynamic Antimicrobial Therapy: A Review, Materials. 13 (2020) 4004.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, X. Liu, X. Han, R. Godin, J. Chen, W. Zhou, C. Jiang, J.F. Thompson, K.B. Mustafa, S.A. Shevlin, J.R. Durrant, Z. Guo, J. Tang, Unique hole-accepting carbon-dots promoting selective carbon dioxide reduction nearly 100% to methanol by pure water, Nat Commun. 11 (2020) 2531.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Li, Z. Kang, Y. Liu, S.-T. Lee, Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications, Journal of Materials Chemistry. 22 (2012) 24230-24253.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bandgap engineering of coal-derived graphene quantum dots

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Coal as an abundant source of graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Dong, J. Lin, Y. Chen, F. Fu, Y. Chi, G. Chen, Graphene quantum dots, graphene oxide, carbon quantum dots and graphite nanocrystals in coals, Nanoscale. 6 (2014) 7410-7415.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of Crystallinity on the wettability of petroleum coke by Coal tar pitch

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Sarkar, D. Kocaefe, Y. Kocaefe, D. Bhattacharyay, D. Sarkar, B. Morais, Effect of Crystallinity on the Wettability of Petroleum Coke by Coal Tar Pitch, Energy & Fuels. 30 (2016) 3549-3558.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Zhong, Y. Yang, Q. Li, B. Xu, T. Jiang, Coal tar pitch and molasses blended binder for production of formed coal briquettes from high volatile coal, Fuel Processing Technology. 157 (2017) 12-19.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. ruce Logan, S. Cheng, V. Watson, G. Estadt, Graphite Fiber Brush Anodes for Increased Power Production in Air-Cathode Microbial Fuel Cells, Environ. Sci. Technol. 41 (2007) 3341-3346.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.-W. Kim, J.G. Hwan, Carbon Nanotube Growth on Invar Alloy using Coal Tar Pitch, Journal of The Korean Institute of Surface Engineering. 50 (2017) 516-522.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhang, Q. Liu, H. He, F. Shi, G. Huang, B. Xing, J. Jia, C. Zhang, Coal tar pitch as natural carbon quantum dots decorated on TiO2 for visible light photodegradation of rhodamine B, Carbon. 152 (2019) 284-294.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Alexander, G. Knopp, A. Dotsch, A. Wieland, T. Schwartz, Ozone treatment of conditioned wastewater selects antibiotic resistance genes, opportunistic bacteria, and induce strong population shifts, Sci Total Environ. 559 (2016) 103-112.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Khuntia, S.K. Majumder, P. Ghosh, Quantitative prediction of generation of hydroxyl radicals from ozone microbubbles, Chemical Engineering Research and Design. 98 (2015) 231-239.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, F. Yuan, X. Li, Y. Li, H. Zhong, L. Fan, S. Yang, 53% Efficient Red Emissive Carbon Quantum Dots for High Color Rendering and Stable Warm White-Light-Emitting Diodes, Adv Mater. 29 (2017) 1702910.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Yuan, X. Zhang, X. Li, R. Qin, Y. Cheng, L. Li, X. Yang, X. Yu, Z. Lu, H. Liu, Great enhancement of red emitting carbon dots with B/Al/Ga doping for dual mode anti-counterfeiting, Chemical Engineering Journal. 397 (2020) 125487.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V.D. Dang, A.B. Ganganboina, R.A. Doong, Bipyridine- and Copper-Functionalized N-doped Carbon Dots for Fluorescence Turn Off-On Detection of Ciprofloxacin, ACS Appl Mater Interfaces. 12 (2020) 32247-32258.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Gao, Y. Lu, R. Zhang, S. He, J. Ju, M. Liu, L. Li, W. Chen, One-pot synthesis of carbon nanodots for fluorescence turn-on detection of Ag+ based on the Ag+-induced enhancement of fluorescence, Journal of Materials Chemistry C. 3 (2015) 2302-2309.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Scott K. Cushing, Ming Li, Fuqiang Huang, N. Wu, Origin of Strong Excitation Wavelength Dependent Fluorescence of Graphene Oxide, ACS Nano. 8 (2014) 1002–1013.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, D. Chen, B. Gu, B. Gao, Z. Liu, Y. Yang, Q. Guo, X. Zheng, G. Wang, Yellow emissive nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots as a label-free fluorescent probe for Fe3+ sensing and bioimaging, Diamond and Related Materials. 104 (2020) 107749.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Song, C. Zhu, J. Song, H. Li, D. Du, Y. Lin, Drug-Derived Bright and Color-Tunable N-Doped Carbon Dots for Cell Imaging and Sensitive Detection of Fe(3+) in Living Cells, ACS Appl Mater Interfaces. 9 (2017) 7399-7405.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Basoglu, U. Ocak, A. Gumrukcuoglu, Synthesis of Microwave-Assisted Fluorescence Carbon Quantum Dots Using Roasted-Chickpeas and its Applications for Sensitive and Selective Detection of Fe3+ Ions, J Fluoresc. 30 (2020) 515-526.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhao, X. Song, X. Chai, P. Zhao, H. He, Z. Liu, Green production of fluorescent carbon quantum dots based on pine wood and its application in the detection of Fe3+, Journal of Cleaner Production. 263 (2020) 121561.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhang, Y. Xiao, Y. Zhang, Y. Wang, Carbon Quantum Dots as Fluorescence Turn-Off-On Probe for Detecting Fe3+ and Ascorbic Acid, J Nanosci Nanotechnol. 20 (2020) 3340-3347.
